title: Geometric window star
subtitle: Cutting a six-pointed paper star with negative space windows
excerpt: The window star is a papercutting design in which the interior of the star is cut into a pattern of open windows that let light through or show the paper beneath. Starting from a hexagonal fold, a single careful cutting sequence produces the characteristic six-point symmetry without tracing or a template.
sourceNotes: Standard origami-papercutting technique documented in Rob Ives, Paper Engineering and Pop-ups for Dummies (2009, Wiley), chapter on symmetrical cuts. The hexagonal fold sequence is a standard geometric papercutting fold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A papercutting technique in which shapes are cut out of the interior of a design, producing negative-space holes that let light through or reveal a contrasting layer below.

A folding sequence that produces a triangular wedge representing one-twelfth of a full circle. Cutting through all layers produces twelve-fold symmetry; cutting through six layers (a simpler fold) produces six-fold symmetry.

content.text: produces six layers of paper, so a single scissor cut passes through all six simultaneously and appears six times in the opened star. This multiplicative effect means small, simple cuts produce complex-looking results, the challenge is precision rather than elaborate design.

Start with a square of paper (15-20 cm). Fold in half to make a rectangle. Fold the rectangle in thirds along the long axis: bring the left third to the centre, then fold the right third over the top. You now have a narrow folded strip. Fold the strip in half along its length. The result is a thick wedge, one-sixth of the final circle. Crease all folds firmly with the bone folder.

On the wedge, the outer curved edge will become the star points. Cut a shallow V into the outer edge of the wedge from both sides, meeting at a point. The angle of the V determines how sharp or blunt the star points are. A 60° V gives a balanced six-pointed star; a shallower V (30°) gives longer, sharper points.

into the body of the wedge: small diamond shapes, triangles, or a single elongated slot. Each cut must be entirely within the folded area, do not cut through any fold-line edge, or the star will fall into pieces when opened. Leave at least 3 mm of paper between each window and the edges of the wedge.

Unfold the star carefully, peeling the layers apart one at a time from the innermost fold outward. Press flat under the bone folder on a clean surface. The star can be mounted on a contrasting backing sheet (dark paper behind a white cut, or white behind a coloured cut) to make the windows visible. Applied with a thin coat of PVA around the outer points only, the star lies flat on the backing without adhesive obscuring the window pattern.
